subtopics.title: What's in the Box
subtopics.content: Official component list from the provided Catan base rules: 19 terrain hexes (tiles), 6 sea frame pieces, 9 harbor pieces, 18 circular number tokens, 95 resource cards (brick, grain, lumber, ore, wool), 25 development cards (14 Knight cards, 6 Progress cards, 5 Victory Point cards), 4 Building Costs cards, 2 special cards (Longest Road and Largest Army), 16 cities (4 of each color), 20 settlements (5 of each color), 60 roads (15 of each color), 2 dice (1 yellow and 1 red), 1 robber, and 1 Game Rules & Almanac booklet.

subtopics.title: Board Layout
subtopics.content: 1) Assemble the island frame by matching the numbers on the 6 frame pieces (1-1, 2-2, etc.). 2) Place the 19 terrain hexes inside the frame. For your first game, use the beginner map shown in the rules; for experienced play, use variable setup from the Almanac. 3) Place the 18 number tokens on top of designated terrain hexes (beginner map) or per variable setup rules. 4) Place the robber on the desert. 5) Put the Longest Road and Largest Army special cards beside the board. 6) Sort resource cards into 5 face-up supply stacks near the board. 7) Shuffle development cards and place them face down as a draw pile.

subtopics.title: Player Setup
subtopics.content: Each player chooses a color and takes exactly 5 settlements, 4 cities, and 15 roads of that color, plus the matching Building Costs card. During setup, each player places 2 settlements and 2 roads according to the setup procedure being used. Keep all remaining buildings and roads in front of you as your personal supply. In 3-player beginner setup, the red starting position is not used.

subtopics.title: Starting the Game
subtopics.content: If using beginner setup, place initial settlements/roads as shown on the official beginner map. Then take starting resources for hexes adjacent to the starred starting settlements indicated in the rules illustration. If not using the special experienced start sequence, the oldest player goes first. Play proceeds clockwise from there. Players keep their resource cards hidden in hand.

subtopics.title: Turn Structure
subtopics.content:
On your turn, resolve the phases in this order: 1) Roll for resource production (applies to all players). 2) Trade (domestic and/or maritime). 3) Build and optionally play development cards. 4) Pass the dice clockwise. 

Detailed flow: 
1. Roll both dice. 
2. If result is not 7, each player with settlements/cities adjacent to matching number hexes takes resources (settlement = 1 card, city = 2 cards). 
3. If result is 7, skip production and resolve robber/discard procedure. 
4. Active player may trade with other players (domestic) and/or bank/harbors (maritime). 
5. Active player may build roads, settlements, cities, buy development cards, and play at most one eligible development card type according to timing rules. 
6. Turn ends; next player clockwise begins.

subtopics.title: Actions & Options
subtopics.content:
RESOURCE PRODUCTION 
- Settlements and cities must be on intersections (corners), roads on edges (one road per edge). 
- On a production roll, each adjacent settlement gains 1 resource card of that hex type; each adjacent city gains 2. 
- If bank supply does not have enough of a produced resource to satisfy everyone, no one receives that resource for that roll (except edge case noted in full rules where shortage affects only one player context). 

TRADING 
A) Domestic Trade: only the active player may trade with others. Other players cannot trade among themselves on that turn. 
B) Maritime Trade: active player may always trade 4 identical resources for any 1 resource from bank. If player has settlement/city on a harbor, they may use improved rate: 3:1 generic harbor, or 2:1 specific harbor matching the shown resource. 

BUILDING COSTS AND RESTRICTIONS 
- Build Road (cost: Brick + Lumber): must connect to one of your existing roads, settlements, or cities. 
- Build Settlement (cost: Brick + Lumber + Wool + Grain): must connect to your own road and obey Distance Rule. 
- Build City (cost: 3 Ore + 2 Grain): upgrade one of your existing settlements; return settlement piece to your supply and replace with city piece. 
- Buy Development Card (cost: Ore + Wool + Grain): draw top development card from deck; keep hidden until used/revealed by rule timing. 

PIECE LIMITS 
You cannot build beyond your component supply: max 15 roads, 5 settlements, 4 cities on board for your color. 

DEVELOPMENT CARD TIMING 
- You may usually play 1 development card per turn. 
- A development card bought this turn cannot usually be played this turn. 
- Exception: Victory Point cards can be revealed on your turn (including purchase turn) if needed to prove 10+ VP win. 
- Progress cards resolve their text and are then removed from the game. 
- Knight cards are played face up in front of you after resolving robber move/steal.

subtopics.title: Special Mechanics
subtopics.content:
DISTANCE RULE 
To place a settlement, all three adjacent intersections must be empty of any settlement/city (yours or opponents’). This is one of the most important spacing rules in Catan and creates strategic blocking and race pressure. 

ROBBER AND ROLLING A 7 
If a 7 is rolled: 
1) No resource production occurs. 
2) Every player with more than 7 resource cards discards half (rounded down). 
3) Active player moves robber to any other terrain hex (or desert). 
4) Active player steals 1 random resource card from one chosen player with settlement/city adjacent to robber’s new hex. 
5) While robber remains on a hex, that hex does not produce when its number is rolled. 

LONGEST ROAD 
First player to build a continuous road of at least 5 segments gains Longest Road special card (2 VP). If another player later builds a longer continuous road, they take the card immediately. Because one player loses and another gains the card, this can swing 4 VP between rivals. 

LARGEST ARMY 
First player to have 3 played Knight cards in front of them gains Largest Army (2 VP). If another player later has more played Knights than current holder, they immediately take the card. 

DEVELOPMENT CARD TYPES 
- Knight (purple frame): move robber and steal like 7 effect movement/steal steps. 
- Progress (green frame): perform printed effect, then remove card from game. 
- Victory Point (orange frame): hidden until revealed on your turn to confirm victory threshold.

subtopics.title: Endgame & Scoring
subtopics.content:
The game ends immediately when a player has 10 or more victory points during their own turn. If a player reaches 10 outside their turn, play continues until a player (including that same player) has 10+ on their own turn. 

Victory point sources in base game: 
- Each settlement: 1 VP 
- Each city: 2 VP 
- Longest Road special card: 2 VP 
- Largest Army special card: 2 VP 
- Each Victory Point development card revealed: 1 VP 

Final scoring procedure is immediate-win based (first qualifying on-turn state), not end-of-round tally. Because of immediate end on first eligible turn, ties are generally not reached under normal play timing.

subtopics.title: Opening Priorities
subtopics.content:
1) Build your opening around both probability and diversity. High-frequency numbers (especially 6/8, then 5/9) are strong, but only if your starting positions also provide a useful spread of resources. 
2) Secure Brick + Lumber production early if possible. These are critical for roads/settlements and tempo in the expansion race. 
3) Place your first roads with a specific settlement target in mind. Aimless road building wastes scarce cards and invites blocks. 
4) Monitor hand size from turn one. Convert resources into board position before you become vulnerable to a 7 discard. 
5) Evaluate harbor routes during setup, not just late game. A 2:1 or 3:1 trade outlet can salvage skewed production and stabilize your economy.

subtopics.title: Common Mistakes
subtopics.content:
1) Hoarding too many cards and getting punished by a 7 discard. 
2) Underestimating the Distance Rule and discovering your road endpoint is no longer legal for a settlement. 
3) Trading efficiently in isolation but tactically poorly—giving a near-winning opponent exactly what they need to close. 
4) Buying development cards without a follow-up plan (e.g., no knight race timing, no resource buffer, no road/settlement conversion). 
5) Overcommitting to a single VP route (only roads or only cities) and ignoring contested 2-point specials (Largest Army/Longest Road).

subtopics.title: Key Decisions
subtopics.content:
Expansion vs upgrading: new settlements broaden your number coverage and future placement options; cities spike output on your best numbers but cost valuable Ore/Grain. 

Road race vs knight race: Longest Road and Largest Army are both 2 VP engines. Choose based on your resource profile and board position, then commit enough to actually win/hold that card. 

Open trading vs independence: aggressive domestic trading can accelerate your game, but may also accelerate rivals. Port access and selective bank trades reduce dependence and improve endgame control.

metadata.notes: Primary source used: provided Catan 2015 Base Rules excerpt.
